data.title: Zscaler: Strong Double-Digit Growth Ahead
data.description:
By Nicholas Tan, Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

In this analysis, we start our coverage on Zscaler (ZS), a cybersecurity company that offers a cloud-based security platform to protect companies against cyber threats. From 2015 to 2025, its growth has been strong, climbing from $54 million to $2,673 million, with an average of 48.19%. Despite its strong historical growth, its growth rate has been slowing from 56.1% in 2021 to 23.3% in 2025. We therefore assess whether the company could sustain its high growth. We first analyze whether the company has any competitive advantage. We then evaluate whether the company could sustain its market share growth with its early mover advantage. Lastly, we assess whether the company could sustain its ARPC growth through its upselling strategy.

Strong Competitive Advantage

To assess whether the company has any competitive advantage, we compile both its competitors’ and its own competitive metrics for Security Service Edge [SSE]. We also further rank these companies' metrics to evaluate which company performed the best. Based on the table, the company ranks highest for both Malware Block Rate and Malware Evasion Resistance, showing that the company’s SSE product is more competitive than its competitors in protecting against malware. The company also ranks the highest for both Exploit Block Rate and Exploit Evasion Resistance, indicating that its SSE product is also competitive in protecting against exploits. All the companies also tie for Transport Layer Security [TLS] and Secure Sockets Layer [SSL], showing that all companies can successfully inspect and decrypt traffic that has prohibited content. The company overall performed the best out of its competitors, as it ranked first across all metrics.

We believe one key reason for its high performance could be attributable to its internal R&D. Based on our analysis, we found that Zscaler’s 10-year average R&D % revenue of 23.3% is higher than Palo Alto’s (PANW) (21.9%), Fortinet’s (FTNT) (12.8%), and Cisco’s (CSCO) (13.5%), but lower than Cloudflare’s (NET) (27.8%). While Zscaler’s 10-year average R&D % revenue is lower than Cloudflare’s, Cloudflare is a Content Delivery Network [CDN] provider, whereby Cloudflare focuses on delivering content across servers rather than focusing fully on Zero Trust.

We previously highlighted that Cloudflare has the 2nd highest market share by revenue in the CDN market and the highest market share by CDN usage of the entire internet, which suggests Cloudflare’s R&D is spread across both CDN and Zero Trust, rather than specifically on Zero Trust architecture as Zscaler is.

We thus believe Zscaler’s high internal R&D to improve Zscaler’s Zero Trust architecture could explain Zscaler’s high performance metrics. Zscaler also has an in-house R&D team, ThreatLabz, to research “emerging security issues” and “eliminate threats across” its cloud platform. Importantly, Zscaler uses the threat intelligence generated by ThreatLabz to review its products across the product development cycle, which we believe has contributed to its high-performance metrics. Apart from internal R&D, Zscaler’s customer base consists of “approximately 40% of the Forbes Global 2000 and over 45% of Fortune 500 companies”. Due to its large customer base, we believe this enables them to gain access to large volumes of data to train their models. In its Q2 FY2026 earnings transcript, management also indicated that it is “processing more than 500 billion transactions every day, more than 20x the number of daily Google searches. This proprietary anonymized data is used to train our AI engine”.

We determine that the company is highly competitive, and we further expect the company to maintain its competitiveness due to its high internal R&D spending. We believe its high R&D spending would continuously improve its Zero Trust architecture and sustain its long-term competitiveness. Based on its 2025 annual report, the company also indicated that they plan to continue investing in R&D, as it's one of its core strategies, which we believe bodes well for its long-term competitiveness in the market. While the company did not release its product roadmap, it has been releasing service updates for its core products, which are Zscaler Internet Access [ZIA], Zscaler Private Access [ZPA], and Zscaler Digital Experience [ZDX]. Year-to-date, the company released 46, 19, and 6 service updates for ZIA, ZPA, and ZDX, respectively. We believe these large service updates reflect the company’s drive to boost its product competitiveness. Thus, as the company continues to release updates for its core products, we believe that the company can sustain its long-term competitiveness in the market.

Limited Market Share Growth From Early Mover Advantage

After determining that Zscaler can sustain its competitiveness, we then assess whether the company can sustain its market share growth with its early mover advantage. We see that from 2015 to 2021, its customer growth has been strong, as its customers climbed from 2,050 in 2015 to 5,600 in 2021. This can be attributed to its first-mover advantage, as Zscaler released its Zscaler Internet Access in 2008, which featured Zero Trust Architecture and was much earlier than all of its competitors in our competitive analysis table above to release Zero Trust. For example, Cisco (2018), Skyhigh Security (2022), Cloudflare (2020), Fortinet (2021), Palo Alto Networks (2021), and Versa Networks (2023) all released their products later than Zscaler. Zscaler’s founder also mentioned that he “started Zscaler with the notion that certain users should be able to access only certain applications” and also that “the name zero trust did not exist at that time, but we are excited to see that the market has picked it up”, showing the company’s early product development of Zero Trust.

We believe that Zscaler’s earlier release of Zero Trust architecture enabled the company to sustain high customer growth from 2015 to 2021, as most of Zscaler’s competitors have not yet released their respective Zero Trust architecture. Thus, enabling Zscaler to compete in a market with no direct Zero Trust product substitute to sustain Zscaler’s high customer growth. However, recently, Zscaler’s customer growth has slowed from 24.44% in 2021 to 8.67% in 2025, which we believe could be due to the higher competition, as all of Zscaler’s competitors have released their Zero Trust products, and there are now more substitutes for Zscaler’s products. Zscaler has also now lost its early mover advantage, as all its competitors have released their products. Despite losing its early mover advantage alongside the higher competition in the market, Zscaler’s market share has been growing robustly from 6.61% in 2021 to 15.81% in 2025, which we believe can be attributable to its high competitiveness, whereby the company’s products rank best against its competitors.

The key strength of its Zero Trust architecture is that it connects users directly to internal applications without bringing users to a corporate network or connecting users to the internet without downloading web content. This importantly prevents “lateral movement” in the network, whereby hackers can gain access to the whole network if they have breached part of the network. Historically, companies used legacy architectures, such as castle and moat security, which granted users access inside the network once users had passed through internal VPNs and firewalls, or hub and spoke networks, whereby devices are routed through one central device (hub) that controls all traffic. However, these legacy architectures are more vulnerable to cybersecurity attacks, as they enable lateral movement for hackers in the network to steal data. Zscaler’s Zero-Trust architecture is more likely to prevent hacking by directly connecting users to apps and the internet without downloading web content.

We overall do not believe its early mover advantage would sustain its market share growth, as all of its competitors have adopted Zero Trust architecture, which reduces the impact of its early mover advantage. However, we do expect the company to sustain both its market share and customer growth, as we believe that the company will maintain its high internal R&D spending to enhance its Zero Trust architecture. We believe this would position its products better than its competitors and sustain its market share and customer growth. We further expect the growth drivers of the Secure Web Gateway market, such as the expected rise of cybercrime costs (2025 to 2029F, 11.01% CAGR), “increasing complexity of web-based threats”, and growing use of cloud-based cybersecurity to support its customer growth.

Upselling Strategy To Sustain ARPC Growth

After determining that Zscaler is unable to maintain its market share growth using its early mover advantage, we evaluate whether the company’s upselling strategy could sustain its ARPC growth. We thus compile its historical ARPC by dividing revenue by customers. Based on its 2025 annual report, the company uses a “land-and-expand” growth strategy, whereby they sell additional solutions and premium bundles with more functionality to existing customers. This shows that the company’s strategy is indeed focused on upselling. Based on the table above, its ARPC growth has been strong, climbing from $0.026 million in 2015 to $0.284 million in 2025, with a high average growth rate of 27%. Recently, however, its ARPC growth has been slowing from 35% in 2022 to 13% in 2025, which could be due to higher competition, as all its competitors (from the competitive analysis table) released their Zero Trust products between 2020 and 2023.

Based on its investor presentation, the company’s upsell strategy for its customers includes selling ZIA, Data Protection, ZPA, and ZDX together to achieve $175 in ARR per user. ZIA and ZPA complement each other, as ZPA directly connects users to internal apps, while ZIA safely connects users to the internet. This enables users to more safely access both the internal and external networks, which highlights ZIA and ZPA’s complementary product strengths across these networks. Whereas without one or the other, the user would be unable to either safely browse the internet or access internal applications. To add on, ZDX works well with customers who purchase both ZIA and ZPA for their users, as ZDX enables customers to track network performance across both ZIA and ZPA users. Whereas if customers purchase only either ZIA or ZPA, ZDX would be less effective, as it's only monitoring either internal apps or the internet. For users with both ZIA and ZPA, the data protection product can encrypt and protect data across both internal and external networks, making the product more effective compared to users with either ZIA or ZPA only. In terms of workload protection, workload communication connects users securely to the cloud and its services, while workload segmentation prevents lateral movement if users on the cloud become compromised. Users doing workloads on the cloud can also benefit from cloud security posture management [CSPM], as the product proactively detects and monitors cloud risks. We believe that upselling these highly complementary products is a key reason for its high ARPC growth, as customers benefit more from purchasing all these products together for its users, rather than purchasing standalone products.

* Represents the average of other competitors' products, due to a lack of data.

To assess whether its products, features, and pricing could support its future ARPC growth, we compile both its competitors and Zscaler’s Zero Trust service’s number of products, features, and pricing. We further rank these companies to analyze Zscaler’s performance. Based on the table above, the company has the highest number of products and the 2nd highest number of features. We believe this shows that the company has more product and feature upselling opportunities than its competitors to sustain its high ARPC growth.

We overall expect that the company’s ARPC growth will be strong, driven by its upselling strategy for products that are complementary to each other, positioning the company better to upsell more products to customers. Its investor presentation also shows that there is more than a “6x upsell opportunity on ZIA and ZPA alone with existing customers”, which shows that as customers adopt their products, their potential ARR with existing customers could increase by 6x, which we believe bodes well for their ARPC growth. In its Q4 2025 earnings transcript, the company also highlighted that “logos, sitting at about 45% of Fortune 500 or nearly 40% of Global 2000…… the platform is so big that we can keep on upselling and upselling”, which shows both the company’s optimism in upselling products to its large customer base and the large upselling opportunity. We also expect the company to continue developing more features for its products, enabling the company to upsell more features to existing customers. As we highlighted above, we believe that they would maintain high internal R&D spending, as it is one of their core strategies.

Risk: High Competition

We believe one of the key risks that could negatively impact the company’s future competitiveness is the high competition in the Secure Web Gateway market. Recently, Fortinet released its FortiOS 8.0 with newer features to improve its competitiveness, such as multipath IPsec tunnels, which use multiple tunnels for more reliable and faster secure connections, as well as SASE outpost, which deploys a point of presence [POP] locally in “customer-controlled locations, such as on-premises, private data centers, or co-location” for lower latency. Versa Networks also released new product updates, such as Sovereign SASE to keep data within a country or region for regulatory compliance, Secure Enterprise Browser to better protect users on the web, and also Inbound SSE to “inspect and secure inbound internet traffic”. As competitors update their OS and increase the number of features, this increases the competition in the market and could weaken Zscaler’s long-term competitiveness.

Valuation

In terms of forecasting, we first forecast its 2026F revenues using the average management’s guidance of “$3.309 billion to $3.322 billion”. We then project customer additions using its historical 3-year average, as customer growth has been slowing over the last 3 years, and we conservatively estimate that future customer additions would reflect the average of slowing customer additions. After calculating its total number of customers from 2026F to 2028F, we then derive its 2026F ARPC by dividing its revenue by the total number of customers. For 2027F and 2028F ARPC, we use its historical 2-year average, as its ARPC has slowed much more significantly in 2024 and 2025, declining from 29% in 2023 to 19% in 2024, and to 13% in 2025. As such, we projected its ARPC growth using its historical 2-year average to reflect the slowdown in ARPC. We then taper down its ARPC growth for 2028F to discount potential future uncertainty.

Based on our valuation of the company using a DCF model, incorporating our revenue projections at a 5-year forward average growth of around 22.3%, a discount rate of 10.2% reflecting the company’s WACC, and a terminal value based on the top Cybersecurity companies' average EV/EBITDA multiple of 25.29x, our model indicates a modest upside of 14.18%.

Verdict

We overall determine that the company can sustain its high revenue growth, driven by internal R&D spending and its upselling strategy. We believe that the company would continue developing new products and features as well as maintain its high internal R&D spending, which would enable the company to sustain its long-term competitiveness in the market. We further expect that this would enable them to sustain their customer and market share growth, as their products would remain more competitive than their competitors. We also believe that the growth drivers of the Secure Gateway Market, such as higher expected cybercrime costs and more complex cyber attacks, will also improve its growth outlook, as this would increase the demand for Secure Gateway Products to protect against these cybercrimes.

We further believe that the company’s upselling strategy would continue to improve its ARPC growth, as its products are highly complementary. Alongside its large customer base of 45% of Fortune 500 or nearly 40% of Global 2000 and many products and features, we believe that the company will be able to continue selling its products to customers. Based on our DCF valuation, we rate the company as a Buy with a price target of $139.56. This is despite a 44.59% decline in stock price from January 2nd, 2026 to April 9th, 2026, which made the stock significantly cheaper. We believe this discrepancy could be because its valuation ratio was previously stretched, as its 5-year average TTM P/S of 22.16x is much higher than the top Cybersecurity companies’ 5-year average TTM P/S of 7.89x, excluding outliers.

Recently, its P/S ratio declined from 16.49x on 31 July 2025 to 6.42x in 9th April, 2026 alongside a 57.20% stock price decline across this period. We believe this shows that while its stock price has become cheaper, the company was previously very expensive compared to its competitors, which mitigated the positive impact of lower stock prices on valuation.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.


Wall Street’s major averages posted big weekly gains, surging to new highs for three consecutive trading days, as positive developments in the Middle East—including a temporary ceasefire deal between the involved countries—raised investors’ enthusiasm.

Iran’s regime declared the Strait of Hormuz is “completely open” for all commercial vessels on Friday for the rest of the 10-day ceasefire in Lebanon. U.S. President Donald Trump, however, said the naval blockade on Iranian ports will remain in effect. He also said that Iran agreed to suspend its nuclear program indefinitely.

Both countries are expected to return to Pakistan next week for a second round of peace talks, media reports indicated, citing two senior Pakistani officials involved in mediating the peace deal. Other reports have deemed the Strait of Hormuz “remains effectively closed,” despite Iran’s announcement.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) decreased around 11.4% this week to settle around $85, and Brent futures (CO1:COM) also dropped 3.5% to $91 per barrel as of post-market Friday.

In economic news, both the March U.S. Producer Price Index and core PPI, which excludes foods and energy, came in below consensus. In addition, U.S. export prices climbed faster than U.S. import prices in March, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index, on the other hand, unexpectedly rose in April.

The S&P 500 (SP500) accomplished its first close above 7,100 on Friday, and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) extended its winning streak to 13 consecutive days, which is the longest since 1992.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) gained 4.5%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) rose 6.8%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) added 3.2%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

What bubble?

The AI industry continues to draw billions of dollars in funding, with investors shrugging off fears of a potential bubble and doubling down on the burgeoning technology. Investors poured $300B into 6,000 startups globally in Q1 2026, more than double the previous quarter. And most of these investments disproportionately went to AI startups, particularly in the U.S. Read more.

data.title: ASML: Lower Margins Are The Best News Investors Could Get
data.description:
Shares of ASML (ASML) declined around 5% after reporting their Q1 2026 earnings and are now flat since my last report. Semiconductor and semiconductor equipment stocks tend to react heavily in either direction based on small snippets of good or bad items in an earnings release. In this report, I discuss the earnings and outlook highlighting the good and the bad for ASML. I will also provide an update to my price target in support of my buy rating.

Another Boring Quarter For ASML But Boring Is Good

ASML reported sales of €8.8 billion, whereas the company guided for €8.2-€8.9 billion in sales. So, the guidance was met near the high end of the range. Gross margins were 53%, which matches the high end of the range with operating margins being 36%. Installed Base Management sales were €2.5 billion and accounted for 28.4% of sales. For reference purposes; year-on-year sales growth was 26% and margins contracted by one point to 54% while free cash flow was negative €2.6 billion compared to negative €0.5 billion a year ago. So, the seemingly bad parts are the reduction in gross margins as well as the widened free cash flow burn. At the same time, we note that ASML is ramping up production and that means that utilization is not at full capacity now and that pressures margins while timing of receipts can result in free cash flow fluctuations. Investors tend to see this as negative, but they really aren’t. There are margin and cash pressures today to facilitate tomorrow’s revenue and cash flow build out.

By geographic region, we note that China accounted for less than 20% of the revenues compared to 36% in the prior quarter. This is driven by higher shipments in ArFi and EUV to which China has no access. By end-use, we saw parity between logic and memory. EUV accounted for 66% of the revenues compared to 48% in the prior quarter. This is not a major surprise given that EUV is priced at 3x-8x of an ArFi system.

ASML Guidance Shows Backloaded Delivery Profile

ASML has guided for €8.4-€9 billion in sales marking 12% growth year-on-year including €2.5 billion in installed base management sales and margins of 51%-52% compared to 54% a year ago. For the full year, the company has lifted its revenue guidance from €34-€39 billion to €36-€40 billion. That is a welcome lift to the guidance as it shows that ASML’s ability to supply more to the market and perhaps at higher sales prices though I think higher volumes are driving the increase mostly. What may have disappointed investors is that there is no sign of operating leverage in the gross margins and, as I noted in a comment for Seeking Alpha News, the guidance update merely reflects what analysts had already modelled into their 2026 expectations. That may result in updated estimates skewing higher again, so it is not necessarily a bad thing, but it does mean that the guidance lift is simply a satisfactory one for analysts and investors.

How Good And Bad Hook Into Each Other

ASML tends to be boring in terms of quarterly results. It rarely includes any surprises, and that is basically because they have extremely strong visibility in their revenues. So, while investors at times may expect more than just a boring match of the guidance I do believe the only time you will see a deviation from the guidance is when ASML actually misses the guidance. The seemingly bad items are widening free cash flow and lower gross margins. We note that free cash flows can vary due to the timing of receipts and working capital build. Gross margins are currently pressured not by product mix, but by ramp-up costs that are being absorbed.

For 2026, the guidance has been increased, and that is good, but it merely matches prior analyst expectations. Management hinted that the guidance increase was driven by a stronger immersion outlook driving additional EUV upgrades through installed base management sales and non-EUV growth, which was previously expected to be flat. The margin guidance remained stable despite the revenue guidance lift, and that may make investors wonder where the operating leverage is. I am not too concerned about the margin lift being absent at this point for the simple reason that the company is absorbing ramp up costs.

We note that ASML stopped providing quantified order intake, and that is a big change. I believe it is generally a good one. Far too often, analysts focused too much on the order intake, ignoring timing variability in the quarterly order numbers and ASML’s stock price would react heavily to that. At the same time, it is a very good sign because it shows that ASML is no longer focusing on quarter-to-quarter order intake but knows demand exceeds supply and is building a capacity for 80 low-NA EUV machines. In 2025, ASML shipped around 50 EUV systems. So, ASML is roughly at the mid-point this year for ramping up production with a target of 60 shipments. That is also why those margins look weaker.

The company did not provide any quantified comment on its advanced packaging XT:260 product, but noted they are seeing good traction there. ASML is not providing a lot of information on that but noted the following during the earnings call:

I think we also announced that we were entering advanced packaging with the XT:260. This has been an important tool. We continue to see good traction there. And on top of that, we are looking again at a few more things. Some of them have to do with lithography in advanced packaging. And for process such as hybrid bonding, I think we are continuing to look at the opportunity to support our customer in their activity, and that can be done in many different forms. I think right now, we're looking still at a limited amount of use practically of hybrid bonding, especially in front end. So not too much activity with our customer, but I will say some discussion for the future on how maybe we could help there. So that's the first part.

To me that seems like a placeholder for ASML to consider growing its packaging solutions, which are currently a bottleneck for semiconductor output.

ASML Stock Remains Priced Attractively

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I believe that ASML remains priced attractively. While free cash flow estimates have declined by 15.6% amidst ramp-up costs this year, we note that in the years ahead the estimates have increased by 8% for 2027 and 3% for 2028 meaning that the total change between 2026 and 2028 is slightly up. EBITDA estimates increased by 5% for this year, 6.1% for 2027 and 4.4% for 2028 with a total increase of 5.1%. As a result, I am lifting my price target from $1,634 to $1,742 indicating 19% upside.

While the company is facing ramp up costs this year, we do note that EBITDA margins are expected to continue improving with a bigger improvement in 2027 as production sharply increases in combination with high-margin EUV upgrades as higher average sales prices. The higher CapEx also shows in the increased capital intensity of 22% for this year. In terms of cash and debt, there is absolutely no concern as the company is in a net cash position. The only thing I will keep an eye on are the share repurchases as the current modelling assumes that around 130% of free cash will be returned to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases.

Conclusion: ASML Is Paving The Way For Future Growth

ASML shares declined following the announcement of its first-quarter results. We note that free cash flow burn widened and the increased guidance was not paired with an increased margin outlook. However, given the ramp-up costs I do believe that this makes perfect sense and I do expect that margin improvement will be more backloaded given that the company is set to increase production substantially and it takes time for output to increase while costs are already being incurred. I believe that the stock remains a buy as ASML is basically the enabler of advanced chip manufacturing.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Top AI Companies To Watch In 2026
data.description:
AI is moving from experimentation to infrastructure. For investors, that means watching not just model builders but the semiconductor companies supplying the compute behind the shift.

Why AI Is a Structural Theme, Not a Speculative Trend

AI is increasingly looking less like a one-cycle technology story and more like a long-duration shift in how software, services and physical devices operate. The key question is no longer whether AI will be used, but how deeply it becomes embedded in enterprise workflows, consumer products and industrial systems.

That distinction matters for investors. As AI tools become more reliable, more integrated, and easier to deploy, usage can move from experimentation toward habit. In that environment, demand is no longer tied only to excitement around new models. It is supported by recurring compute needs across training, inference, and edge deployment.

How Should Investors Think About AI Exposure?

Investing in AI through public markets is not always as direct as it seems. Many of the companies building the most prominent AI models are not pure-play public investments, and many public companies talking about AI have only partial exposure to the theme.

At a high level, investors can think about AI exposure in three buckets: infrastructure enablers, AI developers and AI adopters.

Infrastructure enablers include the semiconductor, memory, foundry, equipment and networking companies that make AI possible.

AI developers are the model builders and software platforms trying to monetize AI directly.

AI adopters are companies using AI to improve products, operations or productivity.

For investors, that distinction matters. Developers and adopters may offer upside, but semiconductors can be the clearest public-market way to access AI because they sit at the enabling layer of the stack. Every model trained, every inference request processed and every edge device deployed still runs through silicon first.

Source: FactSet. As of 06/30/2025. These are not recommendations to buy or to sell any security. Securities and holdings may vary.

Top 5 AI Companies to Watch

Top holdings in SMH (SMH) as of 04/10/2026.

1. Nvidia (NVDA) - 18.63% holding in SMH

  • What They Do:

Nvidia remains the most visible company in AI infrastructure. Its GPUs have become the standard for training frontier models, and its software ecosystem gives it influence well beyond the chip itself.

  • Outlook for 2026:

Nvidia remains central to the AI buildout, but the story is broadening beyond training alone. Investors will likely be watching how much of demand is driven by inference, networking and full-system deployments rather than just standalone accelerator shipments. The more AI usage shifts from experimentation to production, the more important Nvidia’s role across the full compute stack becomes.

2. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSM) - 11.39% holding in SMH

  • What They Do:

TSMC is the leading advanced foundry manufacturing many of the chips powering the AI ecosystem. It sits at a critical point in the value chain, producing processors designed by many of the industry’s most important fabless companies.

  • Outlook for 2026:

TSMC is a company to watch because AI demand is not only about better chip design. It also depends on whether enough leading-edge manufacturing and advanced packaging capacity can come online to support that demand. As more custom silicon is developed for AI workloads, TSMC remains one of the clearest bottlenecks in the stack.

3. Broadcom (AVGO) - 8.21% holding in SMH

  • What They Do:

Broadcom is best known for its connectivity, networking and custom silicon capabilities. In AI, that makes it relevant not only as a chip company, but as an enabler of how large-scale compute systems communicate and scale.

  • Outlook for 2026:

Broadcom is worth watching as hyperscalers continue building custom AI architectures. If the next phase of AI includes more internal accelerator development and more networking intensity inside data centers, Broadcom may remain important to the buildout even if investor attention stays focused on GPUs.

4. Intel (INTC) - 5.38% holding in SMH

  • What They Do:

Intel remains a major semiconductor company with exposure across CPUs, manufacturing and broader compute infrastructure. While it has not been the market’s preferred AI name, its position in enterprise computing still makes it relevant.

  • Outlook for 2026:

Intel is one of the more interesting companies to watch because the upside case is tied less to current AI leadership and more to execution. Investors will likely be monitoring whether improvements in manufacturing, packaging and foundry strategy can reposition the company for a more meaningful role in the next phase of AI infrastructure.

5. Lam Research (LRCX) - 4.98% holding in SMH

  • What They Do:

Lam Research provides critical wafer fabrication equipment used to make semiconductors. It sits upstream in the ecosystem, supplying tools needed to increase capacity and manufacture more advanced chips.

  • Outlook for 2026:

Lam is a reminder that AI demand does not stop with chip designers. If training clusters, inference demand and memory intensity keep rising, the equipment companies that enable new capacity may continue to benefit. Lam gives investors exposure to the buildout behind the buildout.

What Are the Key Risks of Investing in AI Companies?

AI remains one of the most compelling long-term themes in the market, but it is not without risk. Valuations across parts of the ecosystem can become extended, and expectations can move faster than realized earnings. The industry is also exposed to export restrictions, supply chain disruptions and geopolitical concentration, especially at the leading edge of manufacturing.

There is also the simple reality that not every company associated with AI will be a long-term winner. Some businesses may benefit from excitement without building durable economics. That is one reason many investors separate exposure between application-layer companies and the infrastructure businesses supplying the compute backbone.

SMH Sub-Industry Breakdown

Source: FactSet. As of 12/31/2025. Index holdings and performance are not illustrative of fund holdings or performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index.

How Can Investors Access AI Exposure Through Semiconductors?

One of the challenges in AI investing is that the public market does not always offer clean exposure to the parts of the ecosystem getting the most attention. Many software and model-layer companies are diversified businesses, and in some cases the market is still debating how durable their long-term economics will be as competition increases and pricing evolves.

Semiconductors offer a different way to approach the theme. Rather than trying to predict which chatbot, model provider or application layer winner will ultimately capture the most value, investors can focus on the infrastructure that all of them require. Every training run, every inference query and every on-device AI feature still depends on chips, memory, networking and manufacturing capacity.

That is what makes semiconductors such an important part of the AI value chain. They are not dependent on a single AI platform winning. They benefit from the broader expansion of AI usage across the economy. As AI becomes more embedded in enterprise workflows, consumer products and physical devices, the demand for compute can remain central regardless of who owns the end customer relationship.

For investors looking to express that view in public markets, SMH can be one way to access many of those building blocks in a single portfolio. The fund includes companies across the semiconductor stack, from chip designers to foundries to equipment providers, giving investors exposure to the infrastructure layer underlying the AI buildout.

In that sense, the case for semiconductors is not just that they participate in AI. It is that they may represent the most direct and scalable public-market expression of the theme itself.

data.title: TAN: Sell Ahead Of The OBBBA Cliff
data.description:
The Invesco Solar ETF (TAN) tracks the MAC Global Solar Energy Index, giving investors concentrated exposure to the global solar value chain: panel manufacturers, tracker and inverter makers, and residential installers.

I am initiating TAN on Seeking Alpha as a sell. The fund is priced for a data center power purchase agreement [PPA] narrative that does not actually flow through to most of its constituent weight. The residential tax credit cliff in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), the 2027 air pocket that follows the utility safe harbor pull-forward, and the backlog deterioration at First Solar all argue against the fund, while roughly 40% of weighted exposure sits in names directly exposed to at least one of those three headwinds. This rating looks contrarian against the last year's gains and traction of the fund, but the current contractions in the industry do not align with the fund's weighted composition, and I believe the next twelve months of earnings will make that gap visible.

Residential Issues

OBBBA terminated the Section 25D Residential Clean Energy Credit for expenditures made after December 31, 2025, with no phase-down period. The Inflation Reduction Act had extended that credit at 30% through 2032 with a glide path to 2034. OBBBA, signed July 4, 2025, accelerated the sunset by approx. seven years and replaced the glide path with a wall. For a typical $30,000 residential installation, the disappearance of the credit represents a $9,000 swing in residential economics.

This issue shows up within the fund in a few ways. Enphase (ENPH) is 6.48% of TAN, the third-largest position. On the Q4 2025 earnings call, CEO Badri Kothandaraman guided Q1 2026 revenue to $270 to $300 million, versus $343 million in Q4 2025, and called it a demand low point. That is about a 20% year-over-year decline, with U.S. consumers having pulled forward installations ahead of the 25D deadline. SolarEdge (SEDG) at 5.65% faces the same pull-forward hangover on a weaker balance sheet. Sunrun (RUN) at 5.24% benefits from the third-party ownership workaround under Section 48E, which remains available through 2027 for leased systems, but in its Q4 2025 report management guided to flat-to-slightly-declining overall volume in 2026 after cutting affiliate channel volumes by over 40%. The above comprises a total of 17.37% of the fund.

First Solar (FSLR) is 10.11% of TAN, the second-largest position behind Nextpower at 10.12%, and it is a good read on the sector's real condition because its structural advantages are as good as it gets in the solar energy segment. FSLR is the only major panel manufacturer headquartered in the U.S. and manufacturing exclusively in the U.S. and allied nations, which insulates it from the Foreign Entities of Concern (FEOC) restrictions that begin to be a concern this year. It collects Section 45X Advanced Manufacturing Production Credits at approx. $0.17/watt of U.S. production, a meaningful fraction of the realized selling price.

However, First Solar's Q4 2025 8-K showed the contracted backlog declining to 50.1 GW valued at $15 billion at year-end, down from 54.5 GW entering 2026. Gross bookings for 2025 totaled 7.4 GW, with net de-bookings of 0.9 GW attributed to customer contract breaches. Management declined to provide explicit 2026 EPS guidance, citing volatile tax and accounting effects. The 2026 sales guide of $4.9 to $5.2 billion underwhelmed consensus, and the company secured a new $1.5 billion unsecured revolving credit facility in early March, which could be a sign that their net bookings have turned negative.

Additionally, the Section 45X credit begins phasing down after 2029 and terminates after 2032 with the final bill. First Solar's 2025 gross margin of 41% included $1.6 billion of 45X credits monetized during the year. Taking this out, the underlying panel-making business operates at a thinner margin. If First Solar cannot hold the story together with every structural advantage in its favor, the correlation to the rest of the fund is concerning

Inside the ETF

The top 10 holdings of TAN are below:

Rank Company Sub-Industry Weight
1 Nextpower Inc. (NXT) Solar Trackers 10.12%
2 First Solar Inc. Panel Manufacturing (Thin Film) 10.11%
3 Enphase Energy Inc. Microinverters / Batteries 6.48%
4 Enlight Renewable Energy Ltd Project Developer (Israel) 6.32%
5 SolarEdge Technologies Inverters / Optimizers 5.65%
6 Sunrun Inc. Residential Installer 5.24%
7 GCL Technology Holdings Ltd Panel Manufacturing (China) 4.19%
8 HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital (HASI) Renewable Financing / Yieldco 4.15%
9 Solaria Energia y Medio Ambiente SA Project Developer (Spain) 4.15%
10 Doral Group Renewable Energy Resources Project Developer (Israel) 3.46%
Top 10 Total 59.87%

The fund holds 35 names, with approx. 40% of the weight outside the top 10 scattered across mid-cap developers and regional European and Asian companies. GCL Technology at 4.19% is directly exposed to FEOC restrictions under OBBBA. The expense ratio is 70 bps, roughly nine times what an investor pays for diversified sector exposure via XLE or XLF. The sector breakdown involves Technology at 43.77%, Utilities at 28.76%, Industrials at 23.22%, and Financials at 4.15%. The Utilities and Industrials buckets capture the project developers and yieldcos, which are rate-sensitive and policy-dependent. The Technology bucket is where the residential risk concentrates.

Tradability

Session volume on April 17 was approx. 2.1 million shares with bid-ask spreads that are generally tight during U.S. trading hours, and the options chain is liquid through weekly expirations out to six months. Fund AUM stands at $1.47 billion. The ETF closed at $55.13 against a NAV of $54.56 on Friday the 17th, a premium of approx. 1%.

Additionally, the fund has returned 95.77% over the trailing twelve months, meaning anyone initiating a long position at current levels is entering well above the 52-week midpoint of approx. $44, with sentiment and positioning elevated.

Upside Risk

If core PCE cools faster than expected and the Fed delivers three or more cuts this year, the duration-sensitive yieldco and international developer names in the fund (HASI, CWEN, ENLT, SLR, and DORL, collectively approx. 22% of weight) rerate higher, and third-party ownership residential financing economics improve enough to partially offset the 25D cliff for Sunrun. A dovish Fed pivot is a large upside risk to the sell rating.

Second, First Solar's Q1 2026 bookings on the late-April earnings release are the most important single-name catalyst. If gross bookings materially exceed debookings and a backlog stabilizes, the stock can rerate aggressively given elevated short interest. FSLR is 10.11% of the fund, so a 20% move in the stock moves the ETF approx. 200 bps before any effect on peers.

Third, the Iran conflict re-escalating to a scenario where Brent sustains above $130 shifts the LCOE (Levelized Cost of Energy) math decisively toward solar regardless of federal tax policy. State-level renewable mandates (SB 100, CETA, and GWSA as examples) continue independent of OBBBA and would accelerate as energy security becomes a national priority.

Lastly, solar has been heavily shorted as a sector since mid-2025, and the fund's beta of 1.73 cuts both ways. A broad risk-on rally, particularly if AI capex guidance in the Q1 hyperscaler earnings cycle exceeds consensus by large margins and flows into power infrastructure names, can trigger short covering that overwhelms fundamentals on a shorter-term time horizon.

Takeaway

A large majority of TAN is exposed to residential credit termination, Chinese supply chain restrictions, utility pull-forward reversal in 2027, or some combination of these risks. First Solar is the cleanest structural winner on the panel side, and its own backlog is shrinking while its 2026 guidance has disappointed. If the best panel name in the fund is struggling to hold its narrative, the correlation to the rest of the book is unfavorable. An investor with a positive view on the hyperscaler PPA thesis is better served expressing it through single-name utility-scale exposure than through a basket that carries the residential cliff as a ~17% liability.

data.title: Aixtron: The Easy Money Has Been Made
data.description:
Notes: Unless indicated otherwise, amounts in the article are in EUR. At the current exchange rate, one euro is approximately 1.18 U.S. dollars. All per-share figures refer to the AIXFF ticker and are in EUR.

Investment Thesis

I have issued several Buy recommendations for AIXTRON SE (AIXXF) (AIIXY) on Seeking Alpha. The last one was in June 2025 (Aixtron: Q1 Was Mixed, But Could Have Been The Turning Point). Since then, share price has experienced a drastic rise of almost 200% in USD. AIXTRON shares are up 330% from my previous article in April 2025 (Aixtron: I Am Doubling Down), and they are also up 110% YTD.

My original thesis was predicated on the company’s strategic positioning within the next-generation power electronics market. Specifically, I highlighted AIXTRON’s dominance in MOCVD (Metal-Organic Chemical Vapor Deposition) equipment for Gallium Nitride (short: GaN) and Silicon Carbide (short: SiC) applications. At that time, the market had failed to price in the structural shift toward these materials in electric vehicles and renewable energy infrastructure.

Fast forward to April 2026, and the narrative has evolved. While the technological leadership remains undisputed, the valuation has reached a point where the fundamental upside is largely priced in. The recent momentum is now driven by a new catalyst: AI infrastructure. While this provides a legitimate secondary growth pillar, the current multiples - with a P/E ratio now exceeding 50x - suggest that the market is discounting a perfect execution of this transition. For investors who followed the initial thesis, the current environment presents a compelling case for profit-taking or, at the very least, a halt on new purchases.

Fiscal Year 2025 Financial Performance

AIXTRON’s performance in 2025 was a testament to its agility in a soft semiconductor market. Despite broader industry headwinds, the company maintained profitability and significantly strengthened its balance sheet.

AIXTRON reported full-year 2025 revenue of EUR 556.6 million (2024: EUR 633.2 million). While this represented a 12% year-over-year decline, it sat at the upper end of the company’s guidance range.

  • Profitability and margins: The operating result reached EUR 100.3 million, with an EBIT margin of 18%. Net profit for the year was EUR 85.3 million, resulting in an EPS of EUR 0.76.

  • Exceptional cash generation: A critical development was the improvement in free cash flow, which rose to EUR 181.9 million from a negative EUR 72.4 million in 2024. This was achieved through an EUR 85.5 million reduction in inventory.

  • Q4 2025 momentum: The year ended strong with Q4 revenues of EUR 187.1 million and an EBIT margin of 31%.

2026 Outlook and Q1 Preliminary Results

While 2025 results were good and above the guidance, what really got the market going was the improved 2026 outlook.

On April 14, 2026, AIXTRON raised its full-year guidance for 2026 significantly. Management now expects 2026 revenue of approximately EUR 560 million (range of +/- EUR 30 million), up from the previous EUR 520 million forecast. The EBIT margin is expected to land between 17% and 20%.

However, preliminary Q1 2026 results showed a temporary dip, with revenue at approximately EUR 59 million and an EBIT of EUR -22 million, largely due to negative operating leverage. Order intake was robust at EUR 171 million, bringing the total backlog to EUR 370 million.

Current market dynamics indicate that AIXTRON is navigating a "two-speed" environment. The surge in orders is heavily concentrated in the optoelectronics segment, specifically for laser applications used in AI data centers. In contrast, the Silicon Carbide sector is facing significant headwinds.

Management confirmed during the FY 2025 earnings call that the global SiC market is undergoing a transition characterized by a "temporary slowdown driven by weaker electric-vehicle demand and substantial idle capacity at several customers". Independent research (Semiconductor Today / Yole Group Analysis) also suggests that the power SiC industry has entered a correction cycle where utilization rates have dropped to roughly 50% for upstream processes, a trend expected to persist until the next major expansion cycle in 2027-2028

Furthermore, there is a notable concentration of demand in China, which AIXTRON management has pointed out in every recent earnings call. While this has bolstered order books, it introduces geopolitical sensitivity. Should international trade regulations tighten or domestic Chinese competitors accelerate their 200mm tool capabilities, AIXTRON could see a normalization of its current premium growth rates.

With that background, and considering the 330% share price increase over the last year, the risk/reward ratio is not looking so attractive anymore for new Buy positions. I recommend that investors take profits or hold.

Upcoming Q1 earnings report

AIXTRON will announce Q1 earnings on April 30. Investors who are not ready to exit may want to wait for this. Preliminary figures have already been announced, so it will be more about the details and the commentary from management.

I have exited my position after the 300% increase. So for me it will be about confirming whether I made the right decision. I will be looking for two things. One, whether the recent surge in order intake is a sustainable shift or a temporary AI spike that the market has already over-discounted. Second, the geographical breakdown of orders in the full report. If more than 50% of orders continue to originate from China, it reinforces the risk that the current rally is vulnerable to geopolitical shifts or trade restrictions. A high concentration in China supports an Exit thesis due to elevated risk; a more balanced global distribution might move the needle back toward Hold.

Risks

The primary risk to this Hold/Exit thesis is a prolonged valuation overshoot. In a market driven by AI enthusiasm, semiconductor equipment stocks can trade at elevated multiples for extended periods. If AIXTRON converts its strong Q1 order intake into record-breaking shipments in H2 2026, the share price could push toward new highs regardless of traditional valuation metrics.

Another risk is the acceleration of the 8-inch (200mm) transition. If foundries move to AIXTRON’s G10-SiC platforms faster than anticipated to combat overcapacity through better economics, revenue could exceed guidance. Furthermore, any breakthrough in the Micro LED segment - which has been a laggard - could provide a new growth pillar. If these factors align, the stock could sustain its momentum, potentially invalidating the view that the shares are currently overpriced.

Conclusion

AIXTRON SE remains a premier high-tech champion, and its 2025 results prove it can generate significant cash even in a soft market. However, for a private investor, the entry price is critical.

With shares up over 110% year-to-date and trading at a significant premium to historical averages, the risk-reward ratio has turned unfavorable. The current market capitalization reflects a "blue sky" scenario that ignores potential digestion periods in power electronics and geopolitical headwinds. The most prudent action is to hold existing positions or to exit and realize gains. I do not recommend new Buy positions at these levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NHS: Too Expensive Given The Unsustainable Distribution And Bond Market Troubles
data.description:
The Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund (NHS) is a closed-end fund that provides investors with a method of achieving a very high level of current income from the assets that they have in their portfolios. The fund primarily achieves this by investing in a portfolio of junk bonds, which are among the highest-yielding bonds that are available in the market. As such, most junk bond funds are able to boast more attractive yields than most of the other things in the market. This particular fund is one of the highest even out of its peer group, and there are few things in the market that can match its 16.66% current yield. However, it is generally a good idea for investors to be somewhat cautious when it comes to any security with such a high yield, as they are rarely able to sustain their distributions over extended periods of time. As such, we want to pay very close attention to this fund’s financial documents and net asset value development if we want to consider it as a possible income investment. This article will, of course, include coverage of these important points of consideration.

About The Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund

The website for the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund does not provide an investment objective for the fund, which is somewhat unusual. Usually, a fund’s website will state whether the fund’s objective is providing investors with current income, total return, capital appreciation, or some combination of the three. Rather, all that this fund’s website provides is a description of the fund, which is:

NHS is a non-diversified, closed-end fund investing in the high yield debt securities market.

The website also includes a statement that at least 80% of its portfolio will be invested in high-yield debt securities (colloquially known as “junk bonds”). Interestingly, the website also states that the fund can invest the remaining 20% of its portfolio in whatever management deems appropriate. However, in practice, we can expect this fund’s portfolio to be almost entirely invested in various types of debt securities. The fund’s most recent schedule of investments provides the following asset allocation for this fund as of January 31, 2026:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Asset-Backed Securities

2.9%

Corporate Bonds

135.5%

Loan Assignments

1.7%

Common Stocks

0.1%

Money Market Fund

2.2%

The only one of these types of securities that is not, strictly speaking, a form of debt securities is the common stocks. The Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund only has equity shares of a single company in its portfolio, Luxco Co. Ltd.:

As far as I can tell, this is a privately held company, as there is no company trading in the public markets with this name. Through an Internet search, I was able to find a privately-held liquor company named Luxco, and a South Korean solar panel manufacturer with the name “Luxco Co. Ltd.” However, it is difficult to see how either one of these could be classified as a Diversified Telecommunications Services company. As such, it is certainly possible that the fund’s equity position is of an opaque company that is difficult to get information on. This would not be unusual, as funds such as NHS frequently acquire equity stakes in shell companies during bankruptcy proceedings that may not actually be operating businesses. It is very unlikely that this position was one that the fund’s management actually made the decision to purchase. The Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund likely held debt in a company that went through a financial restructuring process and ended up with an equity position in some corporate entity as a result of the restructuring.

The takeaway here is that the website for the fund claims that 20% of the fund’s assets can be invested in securities other than high-yield bonds and leveraged loans:

However, in practice, investors should expect the fund’s portfolio to consist almost exclusively of debt securities. This fund is not purchasing equities, commodities, or other assets on a regular basis. As such, its market performance will more closely resemble a bond fund than an equity fund.

We can see this if we look at the fund’s historical performance compared to the bond indices. The most comparable bond index fund in the market is likely the State Street SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK), which tracks the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index. This chart shows the trailing ten-year price performance of NHS against this index fund:

Immediately, we see that NHS underperformed the index in terms of share price performance. This is not particularly surprising due to the fact that the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund employs leverage as part of its investment strategy. As Investopedia explains:

Financial leverage is the practice of borrowing money, investing the funds, and planning for future returns to be greater than debt servicing costs. It’s an investment strategy that uses various financial instruments or borrowed capital to increase the potential return on an investment.

An easy way to think about leverage is by thinking about the way that a home buyer uses a mortgage. When purchasing a house, the buyer will typically put a certain amount of money as a down payment (usually around 20% of the price of the house) and borrow the rest. Any increase in the home price entirely accrues to the homeowner, and the homeowner only has to pay the mortgage. For example, let us assume that a hypothetical buyer puts down $20,000 on a $100,000 house. The value of that house increases by 3% to $103,000. The homeowner now has $23,000 in equity. That is a 15% return on the home buyer’s $20,000 down payment. The homeowner thus made 15% even though the house only went up by 3%. It works the same way for this fund. We can basically equate the fund’s net assets to the down payment. The fund, therefore, makes larger gains when the price of its assets goes up. We see this in the chart above during periods like 2020 and 2021 when this fund’s share price increased to a much greater extent than the index did. Unfortunately, leverage also works in reverse, as it increases losses. Thus, once bonds started to struggle in late 2021, and especially during 2022, the fund’s share price fell much more than the index.

In isolation, neither the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund nor the junk bond index look like very good investments. After all, both of them declined over the past ten years, and very few investors want to own an asset that does not make them any money over an extended period. However, we should keep in mind that both the fund and the index paid out distributions over the ten-year period. In fact, the primary way that bonds deliver an investment return is through the coupons that they pay to their investors. If we take that into account, we get this chart:

This chart shows the total returns of the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund alongside those of the American junk bond index over the past decade. As we can see, investors were actually better off with NHS than they were with shares of the index fund, despite the fact that the closed-end fund delivered a substantially greater share price decline. This is because its substantially higher yield relative to the index was able to offset the greater share price decline and still give investors a greater overall return. While this is nice, we should note that it would have been necessary to use some of the distributions that the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund paid out over the period to purchase additional shares of the fund in order to avoid a decline in the value of your position in the fund. Thus, not all of this fund’s 16.66% headline yield actually represents spendable income. Every investor should, at the very least, ensure that their assets do not run out during their lifetime, and that means making sure that the nominal value of a given position either remains stable or increases over time. Thus, the distributions paid out by this fund need to be split between reinvestment and spending for most retirees.

The website for the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund states:

The Fund will attempt to maintain an average (dollar-weighted) portfolio quality of B/B to Ba/BB, with less than 20% of the Fund’s portfolio composed of securities rated Caa/CCC or lower.

This strongly suggests that the fund will focus its efforts on investing in those junk bonds that are rated in the top two categories available for such bonds. This may be appealing to those investors who are rather risk-averse and are worried about the possibility of losing money to defaults. After all, the default rates of B to BB-rated junk bonds are far lower than those of CCC and below. Every year, S&P Global publishes data showing the ten-year cumulative default rates of bonds with a given credit rating. Here are the most recent figures:

Bond Rating

Ten-Year Cumulative Default Rate

AAA

0.81%

AA

0.96%

A

1.57%

BBB

3.80%

BB

12.90%

B

24.20%

CCC

55.70%

As stated, we see a substantial increase in the ten-year cumulative default rate when moving from B to CCC-rated bonds. As such, the fact that the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund claims to keep the bulk of its assets in BB or B-rated bonds could be quite appealing to investors worried about defaults. The fund’s most recent fact sheet states that it is, in fact, investing primarily in the supposedly safer BB and B-rated tranches of the junk bond universe:

As of December 31, 2025, the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund had 83.2% of its total assets invested in BB and B-rated bonds. In contrast, the fund’s total allocation to CCC or lower bonds was 14.5%. It is, therefore, invested in accordance with its own statement that no more than 20% of its assets can be invested in CCC or lower-rated securities.

We should note, however, that the fund does have to give up a significant amount of income by focusing on the higher ratings in the junk bond universe. This is because, in addition to their higher default rates, CCC-rated bonds have substantially higher yields than BB or B-rated bonds. The Federal Reserve provides the following figures as of April 16, 2026:

Bond Rating

Yield as of April 16, 2026

BB

5.68%

B

6.96%

CCC

13.06%

As of the time of writing, the yield of CCC-rated bonds is more than double the yield of BB-rated bonds. Thus, the fund could earn substantially more income by increasing its exposure to lower-rated securities. However, that would require avoiding very careful security selection so as to avoid defaults. A single default could erase the extra income provided by the other bonds in the portfolio, and it can be quite difficult to pick securities so carefully. The fund’s strategy appears to be balancing risk with income rather than simply trying to maximize income at all costs.

Looming Risks In The Bond Market

Up until the past week, 2026 had been a disappointing year for bonds in general. Over the period from December 31, 2025, through April 10, 2026, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG), which tracks the price and yield of investment-grade bonds issued by American entities, declined 0.56%. Junk bonds declined by 1.00% over the same period:

If, however, we include the past week in the performance charts, we get a very slight gain for investment-grade bonds and a significant improvement for junk bonds:

Clearly, we can see that both types of bonds delivered a very strong performance over the past week. However, this does not mean that bond investors should start celebrating, and it certainly does not mean that bonds have entered into a new bull market. Indeed, there could still be troubles ahead for the bond market. This is due to the very strong possibility that U.S. Treasuries may remain under pressure going forward.

Earlier this week, the International Monetary Fund issued some stark warnings about the U.S. Treasury market. This story was admittedly somewhat overlooked by investors who were more focused on the headlines coming out of the Middle East and predictions over whether or not the parties fighting in Iran would be able to make peace or not, but the concerns of the International Monetary Fund should still be very closely watched and not ignored by anyone investing in bonds as an asset class. This is because U.S. Treasuries are widely believed to be the only U.S. dollar-denominated debt securities that have no possibility of default. As such, at least in theory, they typically have the lowest yields of any bonds available in the market and the yields of every other type of bond are basically set by adding a premium onto whatever the same-length U.S. Treasury security is yielding to account for the possibility of default. Thus, while the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund does not invest in U.S. Treasury securities, we still should pay attention to things that could impact the U.S. Treasury market in order to make predictions about how the fund itself will perform.

One of the observations made by the International Monetary Fund is that the yield difference between U.S. Treasury securities and AAA-rated corporate bonds has been narrowing. From The Edge Singapore:

The IMF pointed to a narrowing gap between the yields of AAA rated corporate bonds and Treasuries as a sign of reduced appeal for U.S. government securities.

…

A narrowing spread implies that the premium investors pay for the safety and liquidity of Treasuries (relative to high-grade corporate debt) is compressing.

At the start of 2019, AAA-rated corporate bonds were trading with a yield that was 55 basis points higher than a U.S. Treasury security with the same maturity date. Today, the AAA-rated corporate bonds only have a yield that is 35 basis points above same-dated U.S. Treasuries. As the International Monetary Fund points out, one possible reason for this is that the substantial increase in U.S. Treasuries trading in the market has reduced the desirability of holding them. Alternatively, investors may perceive that the default probability of U.S. Treasury securities is no longer zero. This could explain why the yield differential between U.S. Treasuries and AAA-rated corporates is narrowing. While market participants still believe that the default probability of U.S. Treasuries is lower than that of the most financially-strong private corporations, they no longer think that it is zero. As such, they do not believe that U.S. Treasuries are as safe relative to the corporate bonds as they used to be. Regular readers will likely know my views on this matter. In short, I doubt that the United States government will fail to pay the interest or the face value of maturing U.S. Treasury debt, but I fear that the U.S. dollars that the government pays the maturing debt back with will have far less purchasing power than they did at the time that the debt was issued. In other words, I suspect that the U.S. government will attempt to inflate away its debt obligations rather than outright default.

Even mainstream financial publications are beginning to suspect that the risks of investing in U.S. Treasury securities are growing. For example, back in July 2025, Forbes published an article by investment manager Dan Irvine that strongly recommended bond market participants to stick to short-term bonds. From Mr. Irvine’s article:

“At current yields of 4.46%, long-term bonds may not adequately compensate for potential risks.”

Mr. Irvine specifically cited a possibility of rising rates reducing the price of the ten-year note as a major risk. However, rising rates are not a problem for investors who hold the security to maturity. After all, the security will still pay back its face value at maturity. However, he does make a point about rising interest rates being a risk even though he does not explicitly state it. One thing that could cause interest rates to rise at the long end is inflation. Should my prediction that the U.S. government will attempt to inflate away its debt burden prove to be correct, then we should expect interest rates to rise, regardless of the Federal Reserve’s interest rate policy. This could be a reason why the yield of the ten-year U.S. Treasury has risen ever since the Federal Reserve started cutting short-term interest rates. We can see that here:

This chart shows the yield of the ten-year U.S. Treasury note from September 1, 2024, to today. As we can see, the yield of the ten-year U.S. Treasury note is higher today than it was back in September of 2024 when the federal funds rate was 175 basis points higher than it is today. The only explanation for this is one or both of the following:

  1. Investors believe that there is a higher probability that the U.S. government will default than there was back in September 2024.
  2. Investors believe that inflation will continue to be a problem, which will allow the U.S. government to repay its debt with devalued currency. Bond market participants are demanding higher interest rates in an effort to earn a positive return in real terms.

I suspect that the second possibility is more likely, and have reached the same conclusion that Mr. Irvine has. Investors should stick to short-term debt if they want to own bonds at all. By sticking to short-term debt, we can avoid the compounding effect of inflation over a long period of time.

The International Monetary Fund also expressed concern that the United States is projected to continue to run high deficits and continue to issue large numbers of U.S. Treasuries. Over the past three years, the deficit-to-GDP ratio has averaged around 6%, and both the IMF and the Congressional Budget Office believe that deficits will continue to stay at least that high over the next ten years. That is an extraordinarily high level for a nation to run as a matter of policy, and it suggests that the just-discussed situation will get worse going forward.

This means that interest rates will continue to be pressured upward, and that is why investors in the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund need to pay attention. Bond prices move inversely to yields, and if U.S. Treasury yields are being pressured upwards by the deficit spending, then U.S. Treasury prices will be pressured downwards by the same dynamic. As was already mentioned, the yields of corporate bonds tend to trade at a level that gives them a higher yield of U.S. Treasuries in order to account for the default risk possessed by the corporates. Thus, if U.S. Treasury yields are being pressured upwards, then the yields of all U.S. dollar-denominated bonds will be pressured upwards. This will push down the price of corporate bonds, including the ones held in the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund. As such, we can likely expect the fund’s ability to earn capital gains by exploiting interest rate declines and its ability to deliver share price appreciation to be weaker than was the case in the past. In short, this fund could struggle for reasons that are entirely out of its control.

The Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund Versus Indices And Peers

As mentioned in the introduction, the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund boasts a 16.66% yield at the current share price. This is substantially higher than the yield of any American or global bond index fund, which we can see in the following chart:

Index/ETF

TTM Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index

Vanguard Total World Bond ETF (BNDW)

Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF (BNDX)

5.04%

The highest-yielding of the major American and global bond indices is the one tracking junk bonds issued by American companies. This is also the index whose composition most resembles that of the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund. However, at 6.59%, we can see that the yield of that index is substantially lower than that of NHS. The yield of a security is indicative of the amount of income that it provides to its investors, so NHS will naturally provide investors with a higher current income than any fund tracking these indices. As we have already seen, too, the Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund has done a pretty good job at providing a higher total return than the junk bond index over time.

With that said, it is not atypical for closed-end bond funds like NHS to boast substantially higher yields than comparable index funds. After all, closed-end bond funds tend to employ leverage. These funds borrow money and use that borrowed money to purchase bonds. The coupon payments that the fund receives from the purchased securities are used to pay the interest on the debt, and the fund keeps whatever is left over. Thus, as long as the fund is able to borrow money at a lower rate than the coupon yields of the bonds that it is purchasing, the yield that it earns from its net assets will increase. The Neuberger High Yield Strategies Fund is not the only closed-end bond fund that uses this strategy. Indeed, most of them do. As such, we can expect that other closed-end funds will be more competitive with NHS in terms of yield than the index funds were. That is indeed the case, which we can clearly see by looking at the yields of other closed-end funds that employ a similar strategy:
